questions.q: What is the primary purpose of replication in fault-tolerant distributed systems?
options: To maintain multiple copies of data so that system availability is preserved if one or more replicas fail
To speed up read performance by distributing data across many servers
To eliminate the need for distributed consensus algorithms
To reduce the total amount of storage required in the system
questions.explanation: Replication provides redundancy; if one copy is unavailable, others serve requests. Option 1 is about performance scaling, not fault tolerance. Option 2 is necessary but doesn't eliminate consensus needs. Option 3 increases storage.

questions.q: How does a quorum-based system ensure consistency during failures?
options: Reads and writes require acknowledgment from a majority of replicas, ensuring that read and write quorums overlap so all reads see all committed writes
A single elected leader makes all decisions and broadcasts them to other replicas
All replicas must acknowledge every operation before it completes
A quorum system automatically detects which replicas are faulty and ignores them
questions.explanation: If W + R > N (write quorum + read quorum > total replicas), overlap guarantees read sees latest write. Option 1 is the primary role of leader election. Option 2 creates unnecessary blocking. Option 3 assumes perfect detection.

questions.q: What is a failover and what must a system ensure during the process?
options: Automatic transition of responsibilities from a failed primary to a backup, ensuring no committed data is lost and clients reconnect to the new primary
A mechanism that duplicates all incoming requests to avoid processing failures
A technique that halts the system temporarily to detect and repair hardware faults
A process where clients manually switch to a backup server after detecting a failure
questions.explanation: Failover automates recovery; it must not lose committed data or violate consistency. Option 1 describes request duplication, not failover. Option 2 is reactive. Option 3 shifts responsibility to clients.

questions.q: In leader election, what problem does the 'split-brain' scenario present?
options: Two or more nodes simultaneously believe they are the leader, causing conflicting decisions and potential data corruption from diverged writes
A leader that processes requests on only one of its two network interfaces
A scenario where a leader node runs out of memory
An election algorithm that randomly selects two candidates
questions.explanation: Split-brain violates the single-leader safety property; multiple leaders create write conflicts. Option 1 is about network interfaces. Option 2 is about resource exhaustion. Option 3 is a malformed algorithm.

questions.q: How does a Byzantine fault-tolerant system differ from a crash-fault-tolerant system?
options: Byzantine systems tolerate nodes that behave arbitrarily (maliciously or erratically); crash-fault systems assume nodes only fail by stopping
Byzantine systems use stronger consensus algorithms; crash-fault systems use quorum-based agreement
Byzantine systems detect faults faster than crash-fault systems
Byzantine systems prevent all network partitions, while crash-fault systems cannot
questions.explanation: Byzantine faults include arbitrary behavior (lying, sending conflicting messages); crash faults are silent failures. Option 1 is false; both use consensus, but Byzantine algorithms are more complex. Option 2 assumes detection speed correlates with fault model. Option 3 is impossible; no system prevents partitions.

questions.q: What is the significance of the threshold f < n/3 in Byzantine fault-tolerant consensus?
options: More than one-third of nodes can be faulty; beyond this threshold, consensus becomes impossible because the remaining honest nodes cannot form a majority vote
Byzantine algorithms require one-third redundancy per node for error correction
A faulty node must receive approval from one-third of the network before its state changes
The threshold ensures that Byzantine nodes cannot communicate with each other
questions.explanation: Information-theoretic bound: f < n/3 allows honest nodes to achieve consensus by voting; f ≥ n/3 permits Byzantine nodes to prevent consensus. Option 1 is about error correction, not consensus. Option 2 is incorrect. Option 3 assumes Byzantine nodes cannot be isolated.

questions.q: In an active-passive replication setup, what happens when the primary fails?
options: The passive standby is promoted to primary; clients detect the failure and reconnect, resuming normal operations from the promoted standby
All replicas simultaneously become primary until a new leader is elected
The passive replica becomes primary but must resync all uncommitted data from the failed primary before serving requests
The system enters read-only mode until the primary is manually restored
questions.explanation: Active-passive: passive standby takes over; promotion is typically automatic or quick. Option 1 assumes multiple primaries. Option 2 requires resync of uncommitted data, risking loss. Option 3 would lose availability.

questions.q: What advantage does active-active replication provide over active-passive?
options: All replicas serve both read and write requests, improving throughput and availability; failing nodes are masked without client intervention
Active-active eliminates the need for consensus or conflict resolution
Active-active replication is simpler to implement than active-passive
Active-active systems never lose committed data, even with Byzantine failures
questions.explanation: Active-active spreads load across all replicas and masks single-node failures transparently. Option 1 is false; active-active requires consensus for consistency. Option 2 is false; implementation is more complex. Option 3 doesn't guarantee losslessness against Byzantine faults.

questions.q: How does quorum-based replication with W=N (all nodes must acknowledge writes) affect fault tolerance?
options: Even a single node failure causes writes to fail, reducing availability significantly despite providing strong consistency
The system tolerates up to N-1 simultaneous failures without losing acknowledged data
Quorum-based replication is impossible with W=N
W=N guarantees that read requests never block
questions.explanation: W=N is non-tolerant; any failure halts writes. Standard quorums use W=N/2+1. Option 1 is false; W=N/2+1 achieves better balance. Option 2 is incorrect; quorum-based systems cannot prevent all simultaneous failures. Option 3 is unrelated to write quorum.

questions.q: In the context of failover and recovery, what does 'consensus on failure detection' mean?
options: Nodes must agree through consensus whether a peer has failed before triggering failover, preventing false positives from unreliable failure detectors
All nodes must detect a failure at precisely the same time
The consensus algorithm itself detects failures independently
Failure detection is consensus-based only in Byzantine fault-tolerant systems
questions.explanation: Failure detection is imperfect (timeouts can be unreliable); consensus on detection prevents false failovers. Option 1 assumes simultaneous detection. Option 2 assumes consensus algorithms don't detect faults. Option 3 is overly restrictive.
